content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street · Suite 620 · Sacramento, CA 95814-2339
(916) 322-5660 · Fax (916) 322-0886

August 31, 2016

Stacey Fulhorst
Executive Director
San Diego Ethics Commission
1010 Second Avenue, Suite 1530
San Diego, CA 92101

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-16-156

Dear Ms. Fulhorst:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding Government Code Section 1090¹
and City of San Diego Commission for Arts and Culture ("Arts and Culture Commission")
members who also serve in a volunteer capacity on the board of directors of a nonprofit, tax exempt
organization that may seek funding under City programs administered by the Arts and Culture
Commission. We are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) [ FPPC Ops.
71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate.

In regard to our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all
pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office of the State and San Diego County
District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.1(c)(3).) We did not receive a
written response from either entity. (Section 1097.1(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that,
for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding
against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.1(c)(5).)

QUESTION

Does the noninterest exception set forth in Section 1091.5(a)(8) apply to a member of the
City of San Diego's Arts and Culture Commission who also serves in a volunteer capacity on the
board of directors of a nonprofit, tax exempt organization seeking funding under City programs
administered by the Arts and Culture Commission?

CONCLUSION

Yes. This noninterest exception applies so long as the nonprofit, tax exempt organization, as
one of its primary purposes, supports the functions of the City, and the member's interest is noted in
the Arts and Culture Commission's official records.

¹ All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-16-156
Page No. 2

FACTS

By way of background, the City of San Diego's Arts and Culture Commission has 15
members and was established by ordinance in 1988 to serve in an advisory capacity to the Mayor
and City Council with regard to promoting, encouraging, and increasing support for the City's
diverse artistic and cultural assets, integrating arts and culture into community life, and showcasing
the City as an international cultural tourist destination. The Arts and Culture Commission makes
recommendations to the Mayor and City Council regarding which nonprofit organizations and
events provide sufficient artistic or cultural value to be awarded funding from the revenue of the
Transient Occupancy Tax ("TOT") that the City collects from hotel guests.

The Arts and Culture Commission administers the following two City programs as a means
of distributing TOT revenue:

• Organizational Support Program ("OSP"): provides funding to nonprofit, tax exempt
organizations for general operating support with an emphasis on the delivery of programs and
services that impact the City's quality of life and tourism. Qualifying applicants must provide
75% or more of their core programming within the boundaries of the City.

• Creative Communities San Diego ("CCSD"): provides funding to nonprofit, tax exempt
organizations for sponsoring community-based festivals, parades, and celebrations with an
emphasis on projects that promote neighborhood pride and community reinvestment, make arts
and culture activities more available and accessible in City neighborhoods, and encourage
people of diverse backgrounds to share their heritage and culture. Qualifying events must occur
within the boundaries of the City.

Among other criteria, a nonprofit organization is eligible to apply for OSP or CCSD funding
only if the relevant program or event fulfills a mission that "obviously aligns with the purpose of
TOT funding, which is: to enhance the economy; contribute to San Diego's national and
international reputation as a cultural destination; provide access to excellence in culture and the arts
for residents and visitors; to enrich the lives of the people of San Diego; and to build healthy, vital
neighborhoods."

Members of the Arts and Culture Commission annually review 150 to 200 funding
applications from nonprofit organizations. They evaluate and rank the various organizations, and
ultimately make recommendations to the Mayor and City Council regarding which organizations
should be awarded funding and how much funding those organizations should receive. Final
funding award decisions are made by the Mayor and City Council during deliberations and approval
of the City's annual budget, and are reflected in contracts for services issued by City staff to each
awardee. The Mayor and City Council are not bound by the Arts and Culture Commission's
recommendations and the decisions on awardees and funding amounts rest solely with the City
Council, subject to Mayoral veto.

You are the Executive Director of the City of San Diego Ethics Commission. You have
requested advice regarding whether the noninterest exception set forth in Section 1091.5(a)(8)
applies to an Arts and Culture Commission member who also serves in a volunteer capacity on the

File No. A-16-156
Page No. 3

board of directors of a nonprofit, tax exempt organization that will seek funding under the OSP or
CCSD programs.

ANALYSIS

Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities,
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Section 1090 is concerned with
financial interests, other than remote or minimal interests that prevent public officials from
exercising absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their
agencies. (Stigall v. City of Taft (1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) A contract that violates Section 1090 is
void, regardless of whether the terms of the contract are fair and equitable to all parties. (Thomson
v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646-649.)

When Section 1090 is applicable to one member of a governing body of a public entity, the
prohibition cannot be avoided by having the interested board member abstain; the entire governing
body is precluded from entering into the contract. (Id. at pp. 647-649.) However, the Legislature has
created various statutory exceptions to Section 1090's prohibition where the financial interest
involved is deemed a "remote interest," as defined in Section 1091, or a "noninterest," as defined in
Section 1091.5.

If a "remote interest" is present, the contract may be made if: (1) the officer discloses his or
her financial interest in the contract to the public agency; (2) the interest is noted in the public
agency's official records; and (3) the officer abstains from any participation in the making of the
contract. (Section 1091(a); 88 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 106, 108 (2005); 83 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 246, 248
(2000).) If a "noninterest" is present, the contract may be made without the officer's abstention.
(City of Vernon v. Central Basin Mun. Water Dist. (1999) 69 Cal.App.4th 508, 514-515; 84
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 158, 159-160 (2001).)

We employ a six-step analysis to determine whether a public officer has a disqualifying
conflict of interest under Section 1090. Steps one through four are not at issue here: (1) a member
of the Arts and Culture Commission is a public officer subject to Section 1090; (2) a contract is
involved because the Arts and Culture Commission will make recommendations to the Mayor and
City Council regarding which organizations should be awarded a contract under the OSD or CCSD
programs; (3) the member would make or participate in making of a contract because the Arts and
Culture Commission evaluates applicants for funding under the OSD or CCSD programs; and (4)
the member would have a financial interest in a contract awarded by the City to a nonprofit, tax
exempt organization under the OSD or CCSD programs if the member also serves on the board of
directors of the awardee.

At issue is the type of interest that an Arts and Culture Commission member that serves on
the board of directors of a nonprofit, tax exempt organization seeking OSD or CCSD funding would
have in a contract awarded by the City to that organization. Section 1091.5(a)(8) provides in
pertinent part as follows:

File No. A-16-156
Page No. 4

"(a) An officer or employee shall not be deemed to be interested in a contract if his or her interest is any of the following: ...

"(8) That of a noncompensated officer of a nonprofit, tax-exempt corporation, which, as one of its primary purposes, supports the functions of the body or board or to which the body or board has a legal obligation to give particular consideration, and provided further that this interest is noted in its official records."

We have previously determined that Section 1091.5(a)(8)'s noninterest exception applies to a public officer who also serves on the board of directors for a nonprofit, tax exempt organization if at least one of the organization's primary purposes supports the functions of the City. (Sullivan Advice Letter, No. A-15-121; Winchester Advice Letter, No. A-16-030.) In the Sullivan Advice Letter, we determined that this noninterest exception applied to a vice mayor who also served as an uncompensated Vice Commander for the Veterans of Foreign Wars because that nonprofit, tax exempt organization supported the functions of the city by providing veteran services to the city's residents. In the Winchester Advice Letter, we found the noninterest exception applicable to a councilmember who also served as an uncompensated member of the Board of Directors of the local Rotary Club because that nonprofit, tax exempt organization supported the functions of the city by providing various beneficial services to the city.

Consistent with those previous determinations, we conclude that Section 1091.5(a)(8)'s noninterest exception would apply to an Arts and Culture Commission member who also serves in a volunteer capacity on the board of directors of a nonprofit, tax exempt organization seeking funding from the City under the OSP or CCSD programs, if the organization, as one of its primary purposes, supports the functions of the City, and if the member's interest is noted in the Arts and Culture Commission's official records.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: Matthew F. Christy
Counsel, Legal Division

MFC:jgl

administered by the Arts and Culture Commission?

Yes. This noninterest exception applies so long as the nonprofit, tax exempt organization, as 
one of its primary purposes, supports the functions of the City, and the member's interest is noted in 
the Arts and Culture Commission's official records.

v. Call ( 1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646-649.)

We employ a six-step analysis to determine whether a public officer has a disqualifying

official records."

City of San Diego's Arts and Culture Commission who also serves in a volunteer capacity on the

CONCLUSION: Yes. This noninterest exception applies so long as the nonprofit, tax exempt organization, as 
one of its primary purposes, supports the functions of the City, and the member's interest is noted in 
the Arts and Culture Commission's official records.
